narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the effort you've put into your application, including your choice to commit to full-time studies. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 5, a Year 3 decile of 4, a middle family income bracket, an undergraduate GPA of 2.60, and an LSAT score of 24—compared to typical students who average higher deciles around 5.7 and 5.5, a GPA of 3.21, and an LSAT around 37. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 83% chance that you would fail it, leading to the regrettable rejection of your application to protect the school's bar passage rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which is notably below the typical student's and signaled lower readiness for the rigors ahead. Next in importance, your Year 1 and Year 3 deciles, both in the lower half compared to averages, further contributed to the model's concern about your academic trajectory. Your undergraduate GPA, while not as dominant, also nudged the prediction toward failure since it falls short of the norm. On a more positive note, your full-time enrollment status and middle income bracket slightly favored a passing prediction, aligning with the majority of successful students. Overall, these elements combined to outweigh the positives in the model's assessment for someone with your background. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, as it's the top driver here, and strengthening your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program. Building a stronger academic record in subsequent years could also help shift future predictions. We encourage you to seek mentorship from Black women lawyers who've navigated similar paths—they can offer tailored advice. Your journey isn't over; many have turned around lower starting points with focused effort. Keep pushing—you have the resilience to succeed.
